title: Preparation of agarose pads suitable for viewing filamentous cyanobacterial microbial communities using time-lapse imaging
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"aicnb","text":"This protocol is for viewing cultures on top of agar pads, e.g filamentous cyanobacterial microbial community (from a freshwater environment) that form biofilms and granular structures, using time-lapse imaging. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"fknun","text":"Materials for STEP 1:Agarose pad medium ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":40}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ebgq0","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bsfpo","text":"Gelling agent: MacroSieve Low melt agarose (Flowgen Cat No H15621) ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cm6ho","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9p5tf","text":"Agarose percentage: 2% ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d1a3j","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"emgvg","text":"Medium volume:  ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":15,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"feecg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"amount","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"amount":"50","error":false,"guid":"D3F1B7F0C7B311EC89D207B15E838F0F","id":0,"label":"","scientificNotation":false,"unit":2}}}}
steps: Theoretically any medium solution can be used to make agarose pads. Here, we describe 2% agarose pads made with BG11+ ( DSMZ Medium 1593 ) Note: we make the ‘BG11-Mix’ as individual components. Into a 100 mL Duran bottle, add 1 g agarose and make up to 47.35 mL with DW.
Autoclave at 120 °C for 20 – 30 minutes. Remove at 80 °C . For accuracy, you can weigh the bottle before and after autoclaving to calculate any evaporation losses.
After autoclaving, gentle swirl the bottle to ensure the agarose is fully dissolved.
Once removed from the autoclave, keep in a hot water bath ( 55 °C ) to prevent agarose from setting.

Working in a sterile environment e.g Microbiological safety cabinet (MSC), add the following sterile BG11+ components: A B 1 BG11+ Top Table mix (salts) 2 mL 2 Trace metals 50 μl 3 NaNO3 solution (300 gL-1) 250 μl 4 BG11-Mix components 3 x 100uL
At this point, there are two optional routes to take:
Optional route 2
Sterilise 20 22-mm 2 cover glass slides with 70% Ethanol inside a MSC, allowing time for drying (~ 01:00:00 ).
Sterilise 20 22-mm 2 cover glass slides with 70% Ethanol inside a MSC, allowing time for drying (~ 01:00:00 ).

Add 50 mL vitamin B12 or vitamin mix ( 0.02 g/L ) to the solution in the Duran bottle. This option is good for making agar in 60 mm petri dishes (Alternative Step 2, p3), rather than pads. We try to add vitamin(s) solution just before use to avoid any potential degradation that might occur during storage.
Optional route 1
For agarose pads (Step 8, below), only 10 mL medium is required per session, thus, it is best to aliquot the media at this stage to avoid repeated solidification/melting cycles of the medium.

Before using any aliquot, add 5 µL vitamin B12 (or your preferred vitamin[s]) and swirl to mix. To melt solidified medium, microwave at 800w for 00:00:30 and swirl to mix. Leave to cool slightly before adding any vitamins.
Using sterile glass bijou bottles with screw cap lids, aliquot 9.995 mL medium per bijou. These can be stored, solidified, in the cupboard until needed.
Keep the agarose solution at 55 °C using a water bath before moving into the MSC.
Keep the agarose solution at 55 °C using a water bath before moving into the MSC.
Place five glass cover slips in each if 2 x 90 mm petri dish.
Place five glass cover slips in each if 2 x 90 mm petri dish.

Working on one glass cover slip at a time, pipette 800 mL of cooling agarose solution evenly across the cover slip (as the agarose does not spread well when sandwiched, try to reach as far into the corners without unevenly distributing the gel). Quickly remove any air bubbles using the suction of the pipette. We found 800 mL to be the ideal volume for this medium but depending on your media components and how viscous your medium is, you may want to adjust this, anywhere up to 1 mL.

Working on one glass cover slip at a time, pipette 800 mL of cooling agarose solution evenly across the cover slip (as the agarose does not spread well when sandwiched, try to reach as far into the corners without unevenly distributing the gel). Quickly remove any air bubbles using the suction of the pipette. We found 800 mL to be the ideal volume for this medium but depending on your media components and how viscous your medium is, you may want to adjust this, anywhere up to 1 mL.

Gently lower another cover slip on top of the agarose solution, to sandwich it between the two cover slips. Quickly adjust the cover slip, if required, to ensure the agarose appears level. Manipulation is not possible once the agarose sets e.g. within 00:00:30 . The warmer the medium, the easier it is to achieve an even distribution, with no adjustments necessary after adding the top slip.

Gently lower another cover slip on top of the agarose solution, to sandwich it between the two cover slips. Quickly adjust the cover slip, if required, to ensure the agarose appears level. Manipulation is not possible once the agarose sets e.g. within 00:00:30 . The warmer the medium, the easier it is to achieve an even distribution, with no adjustments necessary after adding the top slip.
Using a new pipette tip each time, repeat steps 11-12 for each pad.
Allow the pads to solidify, then seal the lid with parafilm and store in the cupboard until required. We had condensation issues when storing pads in the fridge.
The BG11+ pads are best used the day after making, but we found them also store well for 1-2 weeks (and even longer).
Using a new pipette tip each time, repeat steps 11-12 for each pad.
Allow the pads to solidify, then seal the lid with parafilm and store in the cupboard until required. We had condensation issues when storing pads in the fridge.
The BG11+ pads are best used the day after making, but we found them also store well for 1-2 weeks (and even longer).
A B 1 Petri dish (Falcon, Cat 353002) 60 mm 2 Medium volume 7 ml Pipette 7 mL medium per dish, leave to dry with lids on in the MSC for 01:00:00 , then a further 01:00:00 with lids off.
A B 1 Petri dish (Falcon, Cat 353002) 60 mm 2 Medium volume 7 mL Pipette 7 mL medium per dish, leave to dry with lids on in the MSC for 01:00:00 , then a further 01:00:00 with lids off.
Parafilm close the dishes and store in the cupboard for use the next day. Pads can be used on the same day, but you may get ‘drift’ when imaging due to the continued setting of the agarose.
Parafilm close the dishes and store in the cupboard for use the next day. Pads can be used on the same day, but you may get ‘drift’ when imaging due to the continued setting of the agarose.

When required for use with microbial cultures, the top cover slip on an agarose pad should be removed from the selected pad. The pad can then be cut into smaller pieces (if required) or the whole pad placed into a 60mm dish. You can use the top coverslip to help release the pad from the bottom coverslip and slide it into the petri dish.
Add bacteria culture and start imaging.

When required for use with microbial cultures, the top cover slip on an agarose pad should be removed from the selected pad. The pad can then be cut into smaller pieces (if required) or the whole pad placed into a 60mm dish. You can use the top coverslip to help release the pad from the bottom coverslip and slide it into the petri dish.
Add bacteria culture and start imaging.
creator: Mary Coates
